html:
Bioventix PLC (51B.F): €19.30 Primary listing: BVXP.L | Market cap: about €100m

Bioventix is a small royalty business that the market is still pricing like an exhausted diagnostic reagent supplier. That is the misread. The old vitamin D and hormone antibody book is mature, and China is pressuring parts of it, but the stock now trades near 12x depressed forward earnings and about 12x EV/free cash flow while the company is quietly becoming a levered call option on blood-based Alzheimer’s diagnostics.

This is not a therapeutic biotech. Bioventix develops sheep monoclonal antibodies used inside automated blood tests sold by large IVD companies. Once an antibody is designed into a regulated assay, replacement is unattractive because the customer risks revalidation, regulatory friction, and clinical performance disruption. The company sells tiny quantities of purified antibody, then earns royalties as downstream tests are sold. In FY25, royalties were 70-75% of revenue, gross margin was about 91%, capex was £19k, operating cash flow was £7.0m, and dividends were 150p per share. There is no debt and cash was £5.3m at December 2025.

The stock has fallen because FY25 revenue declined 4% to £13.1m and H1 FY26 revenue declined to £6.16m. That is real. But the quality of the cash flow did not break. H1 FY26 profit before tax was £4.85m, EPS was 69.4p, operating cash flow was £4.37m, and the interim dividend was held at 70p. At the current Frankfurt price, the FY25 dividend yield is roughly 9%, and the equity is being valued as if the core is melting with no offsetting asset.

The offsetting asset is now visible. Bioventix’s Tau/neuro revenue rose from £155k in FY24 to £605k in FY25, then Tau/neuro royalties alone increased fivefold to £150k in H1 FY26. These are still research-use-only assays, not routine clinical IVD assays. That distinction is exactly why the market is missing it. The company is getting paid at the evaluation and early commercialization stage before the large approved clinical market exists.

The external market just moved materially in Bioventix’s direction. In May 2025, the FDA cleared the first blood test used in diagnosing Alzheimer’s disease. On May 12, 2026, Roche received a CE mark for Elecsys pTau217, a blood test designed to help rule in and rule out Alzheimer’s amyloid pathology across primary and secondary care. Whether or not Roche’s specific assay uses a Bioventix antibody, the event matters because it confirms the industry is moving from specialist CSF/PET workflows toward scalable blood-based testing on installed IVD platforms.

Bioventix has said its SMAs are already included in multiple Alzheimer’s research-use assay designs. Among the leading IVD companies developing brain-derived pTau217 assays, three use at least one Bioventix SMA. Among newer high-sensitivity platform companies such as Quanterix, Alamar, Spear and Bio-Techne/Ella, three also use at least one Bioventix SMA. That is the wedge. The company does not need to own the whole Alzheimer’s blood-test market. It needs a few successful assays to move from RUO into routine clinical use.

The current valuation gives very little credit for that. FY25 EPS was 145p. H1 FY26 annualizes to about 139p. Management expects FY26 to show a similar pattern, with modest revenue decline in the historical core and future growth from neurology and broader troponin use. At a London-equivalent price around £17, the stock is on roughly 12x forward earnings. Back out about £1 per share of cash, and the royalty book trades closer to 11x cash earnings. For a debt-free business with 90% gross margins and almost no reinvestment requirement, that is cheap even if the core only declines slowly.

The market’s mistake is treating China pressure as the whole story. China is a real headwind. Management flagged local procurement pressure, China-first sourcing, and cheaper royalty-free alternatives, with perhaps 2% of annual revenue at short-term risk and another 3-4% at medium-term risk. Progesterone also loses about £200k of annual sales in FY26, and Siemens troponin revenue ends in June 2032. These are not trivial. But they are measurable against a £13m revenue base and a business that converts most gross profit into cash. The neuro opportunity is not measurable in the same backward-looking way because the approved clinical market is only now forming.

The April 2026 option grant is a useful tell. The CEO and CFO received options struck at £17.75 that vest only if Bioventix recognizes more than £1.0m of revenue from a commercial diagnostic partner relating to its neurological blood test using a Bioventix SMA. That is not a generic share-price incentive. It defines the inflection point. A single commercial neuro partner crossing £1m of revenue would prove that the RUO activity is converting into a durable IVD royalty stream.

The operating leverage is severe. Bioventix has about 12 full-time employees. If neuro revenue moves from hundreds of thousands to several million pounds, much of the incremental gross profit should fall through after collaborator economics and modest R&D expansion. A £3m incremental royalty/reagent stream at high margin could add roughly 40p-plus of EPS. A £5m stream could add closer to 65-75p. That is the difference between a no-growth 140p EPS business and a 210p EPS business. The market is not paying for that bridge today.

Retail context also helps the setup. Bioventix is barely discussed compared with the emerging pTau217 testing theme itself. The stock sits in the wrong place for attention: too small for large-cap healthcare funds, too illiquid for most traders, listed primarily on AIM, and cross-traded in Frankfurt with negligible volume. Meanwhile, patient and clinician discussion around pTau217 testing is becoming active. The crowd sees the diagnostic theme, but it has not found the obscure reagent royalty beneficiary.

The downside is not that Bioventix runs out of cash. It will not. The downside is that the old core keeps eroding, Chinese customers redesign away from royalties, and Bioventix’s neuro antibodies lose to Lilly, ALZpath, Fujirebio, Roche-controlled or other competing antibody systems. If that happens, the company remains profitable but deserves a low multiple. The key underwriting point is that the current price already discounts a lot of that disappointment while paying a near-9% dividend yield to wait.

Base case: FY26 EPS lands around 135-140p, then FY27/FY28 recovers toward 160-170p as neuro revenue exceeds £1m, troponin begins to grow, and the core stabilizes despite China pressure. A 19x multiple on 165p plus cash support gives a London value around £32, or about €36.50. That is roughly 89% upside from €19.30.

Bull case: at least one Bioventix SMA becomes embedded in a routine clinical Alzheimer’s assay with broad IVD distribution, and several RUO relationships become commercial royalty streams. EPS reaches 220-240p within a few years, and the market values the business like a scarce diagnostic royalty asset at 24x. That supports about £55, or €62.50. Upside: about 224%.

Bear case: China substitution accelerates, legacy contracts roll off faster than expected, troponin fails to broaden, and neuro remains a niche RUO revenue stream. EPS falls toward 110p and the market assigns only 10x, with limited credit for cash. Target: about €12.50. Downside: about 35%.

The stock is not cheap because trailing revenue is growing. It is cheap because a 90% gross margin, debt-free diagnostic royalty company is trading near 12x depressed earnings while its first credible new growth vector in years is moving from scientific promise toward regulated clinical adoption. Current price: €19.30. Bear target: €12.50. Base target: €36.50. Bull target: €62.50.
